_metadata.purpose: Tier 1 curated drug-supplement and supplement-supplement interactions for the interaction database
_metadata.notes: Every Major and Contraindicated entry has at least 1 authoritative source URL. Severities use draft vocabulary (Contraindicated/Major/Moderate/Minor) normalized by verify_interactions.py. When type is Med-Sup, agent1 is the drug and agent2 is the supplement. Drug classes use class:id format; specific drugs use RXCUI. Supplement agents use UMLS CUI where confident.

interactions.mechanism: Vitamin K is the direct cofactor for clotting factor synthesis (II, VII, IX, X). Warfarin acts as a vitamin K antagonist; increasing vitamin K intake directly reverses the anticoagulant effect and raises INR unpredictably.
interactions.management: Maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake. Avoid starting high-dose vitamin K supplements while on warfarin. Monitor INR closely if dietary patterns change. Supplement doses >100 mcg/day require physician guidance.
interactions.evidence_notes: Vitamin K directly antagonizes warfarin mechanism. This is stated on every warfarin drug label. NIH ODS Vitamin K fact sheet explicitly warns anticoagulant users.

interactions.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) inhibit platelet aggregation and thromboxane A2 synthesis, adding antiplatelet activity on top of warfarin's anticoagulant effect. High doses (≥3 g/day) can meaningfully increase bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Use fish oil supplements cautiously with warfarin. Doses up to 1 g/day are generally low risk. Inform your doctor; monitor for bruising or unusual bleeding. INR monitoring is advisable when starting or stopping high-dose fish oil.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF) and has antiplatelet activity independent of warfarin's mechanism. The combination significantly amplifies bleeding risk. Case reports of serious hemorrhagic events exist.
interactions.management: Avoid combining ginkgo with warfarin. If ginkgo is being used, inform prescriber immediately. Watch for signs of bleeding (unusual bruising, prolonged cuts, blood in urine/stool).
interactions.evidence_notes: Mixed: observational data suggest increased bleeding risk, but controlled studies at recommended doses show no significant effect. Severity reflects precautionary approach for anticoagulant users.

interactions.mechanism: Garlic (Allium sativum) inhibits platelet aggregation via ajoene and other sulfur compounds. Combined with warfarin, it increases antiplatelet burden and may elevate bleeding risk. Some evidence of mild CYP2C9 inhibition.
interactions.management: Use garlic supplements cautiously with warfarin. Culinary amounts are generally safe. High-dose garlic supplements (>600 mg/day) warrant INR monitoring. Report any unusual bruising.
interactions.evidence_notes: Mainly case reports. Culinary amounts are safe. Concentrated supplements may have mild antiplatelet effect.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) is a potent inducer of CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein. It significantly accelerates warfarin metabolism, reducing plasma concentrations and anticoagulant effect. INR can fall to sub-therapeutic levels, increasing thrombosis risk.
interactions.management: Do not use St. John's Wort while taking warfarin. INR must be monitored closely if it is started or stopped. Switching to a different antidepressant approach is strongly recommended.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose vitamin E (>400 IU/day) inhibits platelet aggregation and interferes with vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis. This adds to warfarin's anticoagulant effect and can elevate INR.
interactions.management: Doses ≤400 IU/day are generally safe alongside warfarin. Higher doses warrant INR monitoring. Inform prescriber if starting vitamin E supplementation.

interactions.mechanism: CoQ10 is structurally similar to vitamin K2 and may exert a mild pro-coagulant effect, potentially reducing warfarin's efficacy. Case reports describe decreased INR when CoQ10 is added to warfarin therapy.
interactions.management: Monitor INR if starting CoQ10 while on warfarin. Dose adjustments may be needed. Inform your prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Case reports suggest glucosamine (alone or with chondroitin) may increase INR in warfarin users. The mechanism is not well established; possible mild CYP2C9 inhibition or direct effect on coagulation cascade.
interactions.management: Monitor INR when starting glucosamine. Report any unusual bruising or bleeding. Effect may be small but warrants awareness.

interactions.mechanism: Cranberry juice and extracts may inhibit CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for warfarin (S-enantiomer) metabolism. This can elevate warfarin levels and INR. Case reports include fatal hemorrhage.
interactions.management: Limit cranberry consumption to small amounts (occasional serving of juice/food). Avoid large quantities of cranberry juice or concentrated supplements. Monitor INR closely if intake changes.

interactions.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation and thromboxane formation. At supplement doses (500–2000 mg/day), it may add antiplatelet activity on top of warfarin's anticoagulant effect, increasing bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Culinary turmeric is generally safe. High-dose curcumin supplements warrant INR monitoring. Inform prescriber before starting curcumin supplements.

interactions.mechanism: Dong quai (Angelica sinensis) contains coumarin derivatives with intrinsic anticoagulant properties, and may also inhibit platelet aggregation. Combined with warfarin, it can substantially increase INR and bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Avoid dong quai while taking warfarin. INR should be checked if the herb was recently used. Inform prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) has mild antiplatelet properties via thromboxane synthase inhibition. At culinary doses the effect is negligible; high-dose supplements may add to warfarin's antiplatelet burden.
interactions.management: Culinary ginger is safe. High-dose ginger supplements (>2 g/day) warrant awareness. Report unusual bruising. INR monitoring is reasonable if high-dose ginger is started.
interactions.evidence_notes: Theoretical based on in-vitro platelet inhibition. Clinical evidence for interaction at dietary doses is weak.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, significantly increasing statin metabolism (especially simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin). Plasma statin levels fall by up to 50%, substantially reducing cardiovascular protection.
interactions.management: Do not use St. John's Wort with CYP3A4-metabolized statins. If herbal antidepressant therapy is desired, discuss alternatives with your doctor. Monitor for reduced lipid-lowering effect if combined inadvertently.

interactions.mechanism: Red yeast rice naturally contains monacolin K, which is chemically identical to lovastatin. Combining red yeast rice with a prescription statin doubles the effective statin dose, dramatically increasing risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
interactions.management: Do not use red yeast rice if you are already taking a prescription statin. If you prefer a natural approach, discuss with your doctor — the two should not be combined. Seek immediate care for severe muscle pain, weakness, or dark urine.

interactions.mechanism: Furanocoumarins in grapefruit irreversibly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4, increasing bioavailability of CYP3A4-metabolized statins (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin). This raises statin plasma levels and muscle toxicity risk.
interactions.management: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice with simvastatin, atorvastatin, and lovastatin. Pravastatin, rosuvastatin, and fluvastatin are not significantly affected. Timing the dose away from grapefruit does not eliminate the risk due to irreversible enzyme inhibition.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose niacin (≥1 g/day, pharmacologic dose) combined with statins increases risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis. The mechanism is additive muscle toxicity, possibly related to impaired CoQ10 synthesis and mitochondrial dysfunction.
interactions.management: Pharmacologic niacin doses require physician supervision when combined with statins. Monitor for muscle pain, weakness, or brown urine. Nutritional niacin doses from multivitamins are generally safe.

interactions.mechanism: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, which also reduces endogenous CoQ10 synthesis. Some patients experience statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS). CoQ10 supplementation is studied as mitigation but evidence is mixed — it may help some patients.
interactions.management: CoQ10 (100–200 mg/day) is widely used to offset statin-related muscle symptoms. It does not interfere with the statin's lipid-lowering effect. Discuss with your doctor if you have muscle pain while on a statin.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort (hypericin, hyperforin) inhibits reuptake of serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine. Combined with SSRIs, this creates severe serotonergic excess leading to serotonin syndrome: agitation, hyperthermia, clonus, seizures, and potentially death.
interactions.management: Never combine St. John's Wort with any SSRI, SNRI, or other serotonergic agent. A washout period equivalent to 5 half-lives of the SSRI is required before switching. Seek emergency care immediately if serotonin syndrome symptoms appear.

interactions.mechanism: 5-HTP (5-hydroxytryptophan) is the direct precursor to serotonin. Taking 5-HTP while on an SSRI floods the serotonin system from both the supply side (increased synthesis) and the reuptake side (blocked clearance), creating a high risk of serotonin syndrome.
interactions.management: Avoid 5-HTP while taking any SSRI. If transitioning from an SSRI to 5-HTP for mood support, a full washout of the SSRI is required. Consult your doctor before using 5-HTP.

interactions.mechanism: SAMe is involved in monoamine metabolism and has antidepressant properties partly via serotonergic mechanisms. Combined with SSRIs, it can cause additive serotonergic stimulation. Case reports of serotonin syndrome exist.
interactions.management: Avoid combining SAMe with SSRIs without medical supervision. If used as an adjunct (some practitioners do this), requires careful dose titration and monitoring for agitation, tremor, or hyperthermia.

interactions.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids are studied as adjunctive therapy for depression and have no pharmacokinetic interaction with SSRIs. No significant adverse interaction is known. EPA-rich formulations may even have complementary antidepressant effects.
interactions.management: Fish oil is generally compatible with SSRIs. At doses >3 g/day, minor antiplatelet effects could slightly increase bleeding risk in combination with SSRIs, but this is rarely clinically significant. Discuss with your doctor.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo has antiplatelet activity; SSRIs also impair platelet function by depleting serotonin in platelets. Combined, bleeding risk is increased. Additionally, ginkgo may have serotonin-modulating properties that could interact with SSRIs.
interactions.management: Avoid high-dose ginkgo with SSRIs. If used for cognitive support, discuss with your doctor. Monitor for unusual bruising.

interactions.mechanism: Fluvoxamine (an SSRI) strongly inhibits CYP1A2, the main enzyme metabolizing melatonin. Fluvoxamine + melatonin can cause up to 17-fold increase in melatonin plasma levels, causing excessive sedation. Other SSRIs have minimal effect on melatonin metabolism.
interactions.management: Avoid melatonin supplements with fluvoxamine specifically. Other SSRIs: use low melatonin doses (0.5–1 mg) for sleep and monitor for excessive drowsiness.

interactions.mechanism: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone secretion, causing potassium retention. Additional potassium supplementation can lead to dangerous hyperkalemia (elevated serum potassium), causing cardiac arrhythmias and potentially cardiac arrest.
interactions.management: Do not take potassium supplements while on ACE inhibitors without medical supervision. Avoid potassium-based salt substitutes. Regular serum potassium monitoring is essential if any potassium supplementation is used.

interactions.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin in licorice root inhibits 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, leading to mineralocorticoid excess, sodium retention, potassium loss, and elevated blood pressure. This directly opposes antihypertensive therapy.
interactions.management: Avoid licorice root supplements (glycyrrhizin-containing) while on antihypertensives. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) is safer. Blood pressure should be re-checked if licorice exposure is suspected.

interactions.mechanism: Ephedra (ephedrine alkaloids) stimulates alpha and beta adrenergic receptors, raising heart rate and blood pressure. Beta blockers block beta receptors, leaving alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed, causing paradoxical hypertension. Combined cardiac effects are unpredictable.
interactions.management: Do not use ephedra or products containing ephedrine alkaloids with beta blockers. Note: Ephedra was banned for dietary supplements in the US in 2004 but persists in some herbal products marketed outside the US.

interactions.mechanism: Furanocoumarins in grapefruit inhibit intestinal CYP3A4, increasing plasma levels of CYP3A4-metabolized calcium channel blockers (felodipine, nifedipine, amlodipine). Elevated CCB levels can cause excessive blood pressure lowering, dizziness, and reflex tachycardia.
interactions.management: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice with CYP3A4-metabolized calcium channel blockers. Effects can last 24–72 hours after consuming grapefruit. Check with your pharmacist about your specific CCB.

interactions.mechanism: Loop diuretics (furosemide) and thiazides deplete potassium, making supplementation beneficial. Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride) retain potassium; adding supplements risks hyperkalemia. Effect depends on diuretic type.
interactions.management: If on loop or thiazide diuretics: potassium supplementation is often prescribed — take as directed. If on potassium-sparing diuretics: avoid potassium supplements without physician guidance. Know your diuretic type.

interactions.mechanism: Iron can chelate ACE inhibitors in the GI tract, reducing their absorption. The clinical significance is generally minor but can be relevant at high iron doses.
interactions.management: Take iron and ACE inhibitors at least 2 hours apart for optimal absorption of both.

interactions.mechanism: CoQ10 has been shown in some trials to modestly lower blood pressure (~10–17 mmHg systolic). When combined with antihypertensive medications, this additive effect is usually beneficial, but can occasionally cause excessive blood pressure reduction.
interactions.management: CoQ10 can be a useful adjunct in hypertension management. Monitor blood pressure when starting CoQ10. Dose adjustment of antihypertensives may be needed in some cases.

interactions.mechanism: Alpha-lipoic acid (ALA) improves insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake. Combined with metformin or other diabetes medications, it can cause additive blood glucose lowering, potentially resulting in hypoglycemia.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose when adding ALA to diabetes therapy. Dose adjustments may be needed. Common in integrative diabetes care but requires tracking glucose responses.

interactions.mechanism: Chromium potentiates insulin action and can lower fasting blood glucose. When combined with insulin or oral hypoglycemics, this additive glucose-lowering effect can cause symptomatic hypoglycemia.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose carefully when starting chromium supplementation with diabetes medications. Have fast-acting glucose available. Dose adjustment may be needed.

interactions.mechanism: Berberine activates AMPK, closely mimicking metformin's mechanism of action. Clinical trials show HbA1c reductions comparable to metformin. Combined use can cause significant hypoglycemia. Berberine also inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2D6, affecting metabolism of multiple drugs.
interactions.management: Berberine should not be combined with diabetes medications without physician supervision. Frequent blood glucose monitoring is essential. Dose adjustments are commonly required.

interactions.mechanism: Bitter melon (Momordica charantia) contains charantin and polypeptide-p, which have insulin-like activity and stimulate glucose uptake. Combined with diabetes medications, additive hypoglycemia can occur.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose when using bitter melon with diabetes medications. Dose adjustment may be needed. Do not use as a replacement for prescribed therapy.

interactions.mechanism: Fenugreek seeds contain soluble fiber and compounds that slow carbohydrate absorption and may enhance insulin secretion, causing additive glucose lowering when combined with diabetes medications.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose when using fenugreek supplements with diabetes medications. Dose adjustment may be needed. Inform your prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Gymnema sylvestre may stimulate insulin secretion and improve beta-cell function. Combined with secretagogues or insulin, it can cause additive hypoglycemia.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose carefully. Inform your prescriber. Dose adjustments to diabetes medications may be required.

interactions.mechanism: Cinnamon extracts have modest insulin-sensitizing properties. At supplemental doses it may produce mild additional glucose lowering, especially with secretagogues.
interactions.management: Culinary cinnamon is safe. High-dose cinnamon supplements with diabetes medications warrant glucose monitoring. Note: Cassia cinnamon (most common) contains coumarin, which can be harmful in large amounts.

interactions.mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble complexes with levothyroxine in the GI tract, significantly reducing its absorption (by up to 40%). This can result in inadequately controlled hypothyroidism.
interactions.management: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours before or after calcium supplements. Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast. Monitor thyroid function tests if supplementation regimen changes.

interactions.mechanism: Ferrous sulfate and other iron supplements form insoluble complexes with levothyroxine in the GI tract, reducing levothyroxine absorption significantly. This is a well-documented interaction affecting thyroid control.
interactions.management: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours before or after iron supplements. Monitor TSH levels after starting or stopping iron. Women often take both and need to manage this timing carefully.

interactions.mechanism: Magnesium can bind levothyroxine in the GI tract, reducing its absorption. The effect is less pronounced than with calcium or iron but clinically relevant in long-term thyroid management.
interactions.management: Take magnesium supplements at least 2–4 hours away from levothyroxine. Morning levothyroxine with evening magnesium is a practical approach.

interactions.mechanism: Soy isoflavones and soy protein can impair levothyroxine absorption and may also affect thyroid hormone metabolism. The effect is most pronounced in infants but relevant at high supplemental doses in adults.
interactions.management: Separate levothyroxine from soy-rich foods or supplements by at least 4 hours. Patients with hypothyroidism on stable levothyroxine should have TSH re-checked if starting high-dose soy isoflavone supplements.

interactions.mechanism: Coffee, particularly espresso, can reduce levothyroxine absorption when taken simultaneously. Polyphenols and other coffee compounds may interfere with GI absorption.
interactions.management: Take levothyroxine with water only, at least 30–60 minutes before coffee. This is a standard clinical recommendation for all thyroid patients.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort is a potent CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inducer. Cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and sirolimus — all transplant immunosuppressants — are CYP3A4/P-gp substrates. Concomitant use can reduce drug levels by 50–70%, leading to acute transplant rejection.
interactions.management: Absolutely contraindicated in transplant patients. Do not use St. John's Wort with any calcineurin inhibitor or mTOR inhibitor. Emergency INR/drug level monitoring is required if accidental exposure occurred.

interactions.mechanism: Echinacea is an immune stimulant. In transplant recipients or patients on immunosuppressants for autoimmune disease, immune stimulation can overcome the intended immunosuppression, triggering organ rejection or disease flare.
interactions.management: Avoid echinacea with any immunosuppressant therapy. Transplant patients and those on immunosuppressants for autoimmune conditions should not use echinacea for colds or immune support.

interactions.mechanism: Cat's claw (Uncaria tomentosa) has immunostimulant properties that may oppose immunosuppressive therapy, potentially reducing drug efficacy in transplant or autoimmune patients.
interactions.management: Avoid cat's claw with immunosuppressants. Inform prescriber of all herbal supplements being taken.

interactions.mechanism: Astragalus (Astragalus membranaceus) has documented immunomodulatory effects, including upregulation of T-cell and NK cell activity. This may counteract immunosuppressive drugs in transplant or autoimmune patients.
interactions.management: Avoid astragalus with immunosuppressant therapy. Discuss all herbal supplements with your transplant or rheumatology team.

interactions.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, increasing blood levels of cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and sirolimus. Elevated immunosuppressant levels increase toxicity risk (nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, infection risk).
interactions.management: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice with transplant immunosuppressants. Maintain consistent dietary patterns; avoid sudden changes in grapefruit intake.

interactions.mechanism: Anticonvulsants (especially phenytoin, phenobarbital, valproate) deplete folate. However, high-dose folate supplementation may reciprocally reduce serum levels of phenytoin and other AEDs, potentially decreasing seizure control.
interactions.management: Folate supplementation is recommended for women of childbearing age on AEDs to prevent neural tube defects. However, high-dose folate (>1 mg/day) requires seizure monitoring. Work with your neurologist.

interactions.mechanism: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) accelerate vitamin D catabolism, leading to deficiency. Vitamin D supplementation is generally beneficial and does not worsen seizure control.
interactions.management: Vitamin D supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) is recommended for patients on long-term AEDs. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels periodically. Bone density monitoring is also advised.

interactions.mechanism: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) in evening primrose oil has been reported to lower the seizure threshold. In patients with epilepsy on anticonvulsants, this can precipitate breakthrough seizures.
interactions.management: Avoid evening primrose oil in patients with epilepsy or on anticonvulsant therapy. Use alternative omega-6 sources if needed.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgotoxin (4'-O-methylpyridoxine) in ginkgo seeds and some leaf preparations can lower seizure threshold. Additionally, ginkgo may induce CYP2C19, potentially reducing valproate levels.
interactions.management: Use ginkgo with caution in patients with seizure history. Seizure monitoring is recommended. High-quality, standardized ginkgo leaf extract with controlled ginkgotoxin levels is preferable.

interactions.mechanism: MAO-A in the intestinal wall normally breaks down dietary tyramine. When MAO is inhibited, tyramine is absorbed intact and releases massive norepinephrine from sympathetic nerve terminals, causing a hypertensive crisis: severe headache, blood pressure spikes to dangerous levels, stroke, and death.
interactions.management: Strict low-tyramine diet is mandatory with MAOIs. Avoid: aged cheeses, cured meats, fermented foods, tap beer, overripe fruit, soy sauce, miso, tofu, and fava beans. Work with a dietitian. Carry a hypertensive crisis emergency plan.
interactions.evidence_notes: MAOI + tyramine hypertensive crisis is one of the most established drug-food interactions in clinical pharmacology. FDA black box warning on all MAOI labels.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort inhibits serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine reuptake. Combined with an MAOI, which prevents monoamine degradation, the resulting monoamine excess causes severe serotonin syndrome and potentially fatal hypertensive crisis.
interactions.management: Absolutely contraindicated. St. John's Wort must not be used with any MAOI. A 14-day washout after stopping an MAOI is required before any serotonergic supplement.
interactions.evidence_notes: St Johns Wort is a known serotonergic agent. Combination with MAOIs carries serotonin syndrome risk. NCCIH and FDA both warn against this combination.

interactions.mechanism: 5-HTP is the immediate serotonin precursor. With MAO inhibited, 5-HTP-derived serotonin cannot be degraded, causing catastrophic serotonin accumulation and serotonin syndrome.
interactions.management: Never combine 5-HTP with MAOIs. 5-HTP must not be started until at least 14 days after stopping an MAOI (5 weeks after stopping tranylcypromine).
interactions.evidence_notes: 5-HTP is a direct serotonin precursor. Combination with MAOIs is universally contraindicated due to serotonin syndrome risk. Clinical pharmacology consensus.

interactions.mechanism: SAMe has serotonergic activity and monoamine-modulating properties. Combined with an MAOI, it can potentiate serotonin syndrome risk through additive serotonergic load.
interactions.management: Contraindicated. Do not use SAMe with any MAOI. Observe appropriate washout periods.
interactions.evidence_notes: SAMe has documented serotonergic activity. NCCIH explicitly warns against combination with serotonergic drugs including MAOIs.

interactions.mechanism: Ginseng has stimulant and sympathomimetic-like properties. With MAO inhibited, the catecholaminergic activity of ginseng can contribute to hypertensive reactions and CNS overstimulation.
interactions.management: Avoid ginseng with MAOIs. Case reports of hypomania and headache with this combination exist. Use ginseng only after full MAOI washout.

interactions.mechanism: NSAIDs reduce platelet thromboxane A2 synthesis and GI mucosal protection. Omega-3s additionally inhibit platelet aggregation. Combined, GI bleeding risk and systemic antiplatelet effect are additive.
interactions.management: Use caution combining high-dose fish oil (>2 g/day) with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food to protect GI lining. Monitor for GI symptoms. Discuss with doctor if using regularly.

interactions.mechanism: Garlic supplements have antiplatelet activity via inhibition of arachidonic acid and thromboxane pathways. Combined with NSAIDs (which also inhibit COX-dependent platelet activation), bleeding risk is additive.
interactions.management: Avoid high-dose garlic supplements with regular NSAID use. Culinary garlic is generally safe. Watch for unusual bruising or GI bleeding.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgo inhibits PAF and platelet aggregation. NSAIDs inhibit COX-mediated thromboxane synthesis. The combined antiplatelet effect increases GI and systemic bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Avoid ginkgo with regular NSAID use. Watch for GI bleeding, unusual bruising. Inform prescriber of all supplements.

interactions.mechanism: Willow bark contains salicin, which is metabolized to salicylate (aspirin-like compound). Combining willow bark with NSAIDs or aspirin creates additive salicylate toxicity and GI mucosal damage risk.
interactions.management: Do not combine willow bark supplements with NSAIDs or aspirin. This combination is essentially double-dosing salicylate drugs. Seek medical advice for pain management if both are considered.

interactions.mechanism: Valerian has GABAergic activity and CNS sedative properties. Combined with sedative medications (including benzodiazepines, sleep aids, antihistamines), it produces additive CNS depression, excessive sedation, and impaired psychomotor function.
interactions.management: Avoid valerian with prescription sedatives or sleep medications. Do not drive or operate machinery if using both. Inform your doctor.

interactions.mechanism: Kava (Piper methysticum) has CNS depressant and kavalactone-mediated GABAergic activity. Combined with sedatives or benzodiazepines, it causes additive sedation and respiratory depression. Kava also has hepatotoxic potential that may be enhanced by other CNS drugs.
interactions.management: Do not combine kava with sedatives, benzodiazepines, or alcohol. Kava alone carries hepatotoxicity warnings. Combined use amplifies both CNS and liver risks.

interactions.mechanism: Melatonin has mild sedative properties. Combined with benzodiazepines, it may produce additive drowsiness. The interaction is generally mild and melatonin is sometimes used therapeutically to help patients taper benzodiazepines.
interactions.management: Use low-dose melatonin (0.5–3 mg) with benzodiazepines if needed for sleep. Avoid activities requiring alertness. Inform your doctor.

interactions.mechanism: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) has GABAergic sedative activity. Combined with sedatives, benzodiazepines, or hypnotics, it can produce additive CNS depression.
interactions.management: Avoid passionflower with sedative medications. Do not drive after combining. Use under physician guidance.

interactions.mechanism: PPIs (and antacids) raise gastric pH, reducing conversion of ferric iron (Fe3+) to the more absorbable ferrous form (Fe2+). This reduces dietary and supplemental iron absorption, worsening iron deficiency over time.
interactions.management: Take iron supplements 1–2 hours before or 4 hours after PPI/antacid doses. Consider using ferrous bisglycinate, which is absorbed via amino acid transporters and is less pH-dependent. Monitor iron and ferritin levels.

interactions.mechanism: Dietary B12 requires gastric acid and pepsin for release from food proteins, and intrinsic factor for absorption. Long-term PPI use suppresses gastric acid, impairing B12 release from food and reducing absorption. Supplemental crystalline B12 is less affected.
interactions.management: Long-term PPI users (>2 years) should have B12 levels checked annually. Supplemental B12 (1000 mcg/day sublingual or oral) is advised for those at risk. Methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin are bioavailable forms.

interactions.mechanism: Long-term PPI use (>1 year) can cause hypomagnesemia by impairing magnesium absorption in the intestine. This FDA-recognized interaction can lead to muscle cramps, cardiac arrhythmias, and seizures.
interactions.management: Check magnesium levels before and periodically during long-term PPI use. Magnesium supplementation may be needed. FDA advises monitoring. Switch to H2 blocker if magnesium cannot be maintained.

interactions.mechanism: Calcium carbonate requires gastric acid for dissolution and absorption. PPIs raise gastric pH, reducing calcium carbonate absorption. Long-term PPI use is associated with increased fracture risk due to impaired calcium absorption.
interactions.management: Patients on long-term PPIs should use calcium citrate (which does not require acid for absorption) instead of calcium carbonate. Ensure adequate dietary calcium and vitamin D intake.

interactions.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits 11-beta-HSD2, which normally inactivates cortisol in peripheral tissues. Combined with corticosteroids, this increases the effective glucocorticoid activity and exacerbates potassium loss, fluid retention, and hypertension.
interactions.management: Avoid licorice root supplements with corticosteroid therapy. Monitor blood pressure and potassium. DGL licorice has reduced glycyrrhizin and is safer.

interactions.mechanism: Long-term corticosteroid use reduces calcium absorption and increases urinary calcium excretion, leading to bone loss. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation is recommended as a preventive measure against steroid-induced osteoporosis.
interactions.management: Supplementation with calcium (1000–1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800–1000 IU/day) is standard practice for patients on long-term corticosteroids. Discuss with your prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Echinacea stimulates immune function including cytokine production. Corticosteroids are used to suppress immune responses. These opposing mechanisms may reduce therapeutic efficacy of corticosteroids.
interactions.management: Avoid echinacea with corticosteroid therapy for immune suppression (transplant, autoimmune). For short-course corticosteroids (allergies, asthma exacerbation), the interaction is less critical but still worth noting.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, accelerating the metabolism of estrogen and progestin components of oral contraceptives. This reduces OC plasma levels and may cause contraceptive failure, leading to unintended pregnancy.
interactions.management: Do not use St. John's Wort with oral contraceptives. Use a backup contraceptive method if concurrent use occurred. Discuss with your prescriber regarding timing before resuming reliance on OC alone.

interactions.mechanism: Chasteberry (Vitex agnus-castus) acts on dopamine receptors to reduce prolactin and may modulate progesterone and LH secretion. This hormonal activity may interfere with oral contraceptives' progestin-mediated effects.
interactions.management: Avoid chasteberry with hormonal contraceptives. The interaction is theoretical but relevant given chasteberry's hormonal activity. Inform your OB/GYN.

interactions.mechanism: Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) are phytoestrogens that bind estrogen receptors. They may weakly compete with or add to the estrogenic component of OCs. Clinical significance at typical dietary intake is minimal.
interactions.management: Dietary soy is generally safe with OCs. High-dose soy isoflavone supplements (>100 mg/day) warrant caution. Discuss with your prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Calcium competitively inhibits iron absorption in the small intestine by competing for DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) and potentially forming insoluble complexes. Both heme and non-heme iron absorption are reduced when taken simultaneously with calcium.
interactions.management: Take iron and calcium supplements at different times — at least 2 hours apart. Iron is best absorbed on an empty stomach in the morning. Calcium can be taken with meals or at bedtime.

interactions.mechanism: Catechins and tannins in green tea bind non-heme iron, forming insoluble iron-polyphenol complexes that cannot be absorbed. This can reduce iron absorption by 50–90% when consumed together.
interactions.management: Do not take iron supplements with green tea extract or green tea beverages. Separate by at least 2 hours. Take iron with vitamin C instead to enhance absorption.

interactions.mechanism: High zinc intake induces intestinal metallothionein, a protein that binds copper tightly. Copper bound to metallothionein is not released into circulation and is lost when intestinal cells slough off. Chronic high-dose zinc (>40 mg/day) causes copper deficiency.
interactions.management: Supplement copper (2 mg/day) alongside zinc supplementation exceeding 25 mg/day. Common in zinc therapy for macular degeneration (AREDS formula includes copper for this reason). Do not take zinc and copper simultaneously — separate by 2+ hours.

interactions.mechanism: St. John's Wort inhibits serotonin reuptake. 5-HTP increases serotonin synthesis as a direct precursor. Combined, these supplements produce additive serotonin loading, potentially causing serotonin syndrome even without prescription medications.
interactions.management: Do not combine St. John's Wort with 5-HTP. Choose one or the other for mood support. If either is used with prescription serotonergic drugs, additional caution is required.

interactions.mechanism: Kava is metabolized hepatically and can inhibit CYP2E1, the enzyme responsible for converting acetaminophen to the hepatotoxic metabolite NAPQI. However, both kava and high-dose acetaminophen are hepatotoxic — combined use substantially increases risk of liver damage.
interactions.management: Avoid kava with regular acetaminophen use. If pain relief is needed, use the lowest effective dose of acetaminophen and avoid alcohol. Monitor liver enzymes with long-term exposure.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose vitamin E inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis and antagonizes vitamin K's role in gamma-carboxylation of coagulation proteins. This can impair clotting, especially in individuals with borderline vitamin K status.
interactions.management: Avoid combining very high-dose vitamin E (>400 IU/day) with low vitamin K intake. Those on anticoagulants are particularly vulnerable. Maintain adequate dietary vitamin K.

interactions.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with insulin resistance and impaired beta-cell function. Supplementation in deficient patients can modestly improve insulin sensitivity. Combined with diabetes medications, mild additive glucose lowering is possible.
interactions.management: Vitamin D supplementation is generally beneficial in diabetes management. Monitor blood glucose when starting high-dose vitamin D. Levels >4000 IU/day warrant medical supervision.

interactions.mechanism: Magnesium has mild calcium channel blocking and vasodilatory properties. High-dose supplementation can modestly lower blood pressure, adding to antihypertensive drug effects.
interactions.management: Magnesium is generally safe with antihypertensives. Monitor blood pressure when starting magnesium. Beneficial in patients with hypomagnesemia or metabolic syndrome.

interactions.mechanism: Some antipsychotics (olanzapine, clozapine) are metabolized by CYP1A2 and also cause sedation. Melatonin can add mild sedative effect and fluvoxamine (sometimes used with antipsychotics) can raise melatonin levels significantly.
interactions.management: Use low-dose melatonin (0.5–3 mg) if needed for sleep with antipsychotics. Monitor for excessive sedation. Inform your psychiatrist.

interactions.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, the primary enzymes metabolizing warfarin and other anticoagulants. This can substantially increase anticoagulant blood levels, elevating INR and bleeding risk. The FDA-approved CBD drug (Epidiolex) carries this interaction warning.
interactions.management: Monitor INR closely if CBD is started or stopped with warfarin. Dose adjustment of warfarin may be required. Inform prescriber of all CBD use including oils and capsules.

interactions.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, affecting metabolism of many antihypertensive drugs (amlodipine, losartan, others). Elevated drug levels can cause excessive blood pressure reduction, dizziness, or syncope.
interactions.management: Inform prescriber of CBD use with blood pressure medications. Monitor blood pressure when starting or stopping CBD. CBD dose and drug dose may both need adjustment.

interactions.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP3A4 and OATP1B1/1B3 transporters, both of which are involved in statin metabolism. This can raise statin plasma concentrations, increasing risk of myopathy and hepatotoxicity.
interactions.management: Inform prescriber of CBD use with statin therapy. Monitor for muscle pain, weakness, or elevated liver enzymes. Dose adjustment may be needed.

interactions.mechanism: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) has been shown in clinical trials to reduce fasting blood glucose and HbA1c. Combined with diabetes medications, it can cause additive glucose lowering.
interactions.management: Monitor blood glucose when adding ashwagandha to diabetes therapy. Inform prescriber. Dose adjustment may be needed.

interactions.mechanism: Ashwagandha has mild adaptogenic blood pressure-lowering effects in some studies. Combined with antihypertensives, minor additive effects are possible.
interactions.management: Monitor blood pressure when starting ashwagandha with antihypertensive therapy. Generally well-tolerated but inform prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Ashwagandha has anxiolytic and mild sedative properties via GABAergic mechanisms. Combined with sedative medications, it may add to CNS depressant effects.
interactions.management: Use caution combining ashwagandha with sedatives. Avoid driving or operating machinery if drowsiness occurs. Inform prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: Curcumin may bind thyroid hormone and reduce its absorption when taken simultaneously. Evidence is limited to in vitro and animal data, but a small clinical effect on levothyroxine absorption is plausible at high doses.
interactions.management: Take levothyroxine well before curcumin supplements. Separate by at least 4 hours. Monitor TSH if starting high-dose curcumin.

interactions.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation via multiple pathways (COX inhibition, TXA2 suppression, cAMP elevation). With anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs, this produces additive bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Use caution with high-dose curcumin supplements (>500 mg/day) with anticoagulants. Culinary turmeric is generally safe. Monitor for unusual bruising or bleeding.

interactions.mechanism: Magnesium is required for insulin receptor signaling. Deficiency impairs insulin sensitivity. Supplementation in deficient patients improves glucose regulation and can mildly enhance medication effects.
interactions.management: Magnesium is generally beneficial in diabetes management. Monitor blood glucose when starting magnesium. Particularly relevant in patients with hypomagnesemia (common in type 2 diabetes).

interactions.mechanism: Calcium and magnesium compete for absorption at intestinal transport sites. Very high calcium intake can reduce magnesium absorption, and excess magnesium can impair calcium absorption. The effect is dose-dependent and clinically minor at typical supplement doses.
interactions.management: Take calcium and magnesium at different times for optimal absorption if treating a specific deficiency. A 2:1 Ca:Mg ratio is commonly recommended in combination supplements. Many people take them together without issue.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose vitamin D increases calcium absorption. Without adequate vitamin K2, this absorbed calcium may deposit in arterial walls rather than bone. K2 activates matrix Gla protein (MGP) and osteocalcin, directing calcium to appropriate sites. The combination is synergistic and generally recommended.
interactions.management: Consider taking vitamin K2 (MK-7, 100–200 mcg/day) alongside high-dose vitamin D supplementation (>2000 IU/day) to optimize calcium utilization. Particularly relevant for bone health and cardiovascular safety.

interactions.mechanism: Beta blockers (especially propranolol, atenolol) suppress endogenous melatonin secretion by blocking sympathetic signaling to the pineal gland. This can impair sleep quality in patients on beta blockers. Melatonin supplementation may restore normal sleep.
interactions.management: Melatonin supplementation (0.5–3 mg at bedtime) may be helpful for sleep disturbances in patients on beta blockers. Generally safe. Inform prescriber.

interactions.mechanism: HIV protease inhibitors (indinavir, ritonavir, saquinavir, etc.) are CYP3A4/P-gp substrates. St. John's Wort induces both, causing a 50–80% reduction in protease inhibitor plasma levels, leading to virological failure and drug resistance.
interactions.management: Absolutely contraindicated. St. John's Wort must not be used with any antiretroviral therapy. This interaction has led to treatment failures and HIV drug resistance. The FDA has issued specific warnings.

interactions.mechanism: In severely immunocompromised patients, live probiotic bacteria (particularly Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) carry a risk of bacteremia or sepsis (rare but reported). The risk is greatest with Saccharomyces boulardii (fungemia risk) in immunocompromised hosts.
interactions.management: Avoid probiotics containing live organisms in severely immunocompromised patients (transplant, chemotherapy). Discuss with transplant or oncology team. Heat-killed probiotics or postbiotics are safer alternatives.

interactions.mechanism: Panax ginseng may reduce the anticoagulant effect of warfarin. A controlled trial found ginseng reduced warfarin AUC and INR. The mechanism may involve CYP induction or direct procoagulant ginsenoside effects.
interactions.management: Monitor INR closely if taking ginseng with warfarin. INR may need to be checked more frequently and warfarin dose adjusted.

interactions.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with renin-angiotensin system activation and elevated blood pressure. Correction of deficiency may modestly lower blood pressure, potentially adding to antihypertensive effects.
interactions.management: Vitamin D supplementation is generally recommended for deficient patients on antihypertensives. Monitor blood pressure when correcting significant deficiency. Usually beneficial rather than problematic.

interactions.mechanism: Both omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin E suppress platelet aggregation via separate pathways (membrane phospholipid remodeling and inhibition of platelet thromboxane synthesis), producing additive antiplatelet effects.
interactions.management: Monitor for signs of easy bruising or bleeding. Consider limiting vitamin E to ≤400 IU/day when taking fish oil.

interactions.mechanism: Both omega-3 fatty acids and ginkgo biloba (via ginkgolides inhibiting PAF) independently inhibit platelet aggregation, producing additive antiplatelet/bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Monitor for signs of bruising or prolonged bleeding, especially before surgery. Consider separating or reducing doses.

interactions.mechanism: Valerian acts as a GABAergic/adenosine modulator promoting sleep, while melatonin acts on MT1/MT2 receptors; combined use produces additive CNS sedation and may increase next-day drowsiness.
interactions.management: Start with lower doses of both if combining. Avoid driving or operating machinery after taking both.

interactions.mechanism: Calcium ions form poorly soluble chelate complexes with the fluoroquinolone ring structure, significantly reducing GI absorption and plasma concentrations, risking antibiotic treatment failure.
interactions.management: Take fluoroquinolone antibiotics at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after calcium supplements.

interactions.mechanism: PPIs suppress gastric acid production; acidic pH is required to reduce dietary ferric iron (Fe3+) to absorbable ferrous form (Fe2+), so hypochlorhydria impairs non-heme iron absorption.
interactions.management: Consider taking iron supplements at a different time than PPI, or use ferrous bisglycinate which is less pH-dependent.

interactions.mechanism: Divalent cations (Mg2+, Ca2+) form insoluble chelate complexes with bisphosphonate molecules in the GI tract, severely reducing oral bioavailability.
interactions.management: Take bisphosphonates on an empty stomach with plain water. Wait at least 30-60 minutes before any mineral supplements.

interactions.mechanism: Ginkgolides (particularly ginkgolide B) are potent PAF antagonists; concurrent use with aspirin (COX-1) or clopidogrel (P2Y12) produces additive inhibition of platelet activation, increasing bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Avoid combining ginkgo with antiplatelet drugs. Discontinue ginkgo at least 7 days before surgery.

interactions.mechanism: High-dose alpha-tocopherol antagonizes vitamin K-dependent clotting factor activation and independently inhibits platelet aggregation; combined with anticoagulants, this produces additive bleeding risk and may elevate INR.
interactions.management: Limit vitamin E to ≤400 IU/day when taking anticoagulants. Monitor INR more frequently if vitamin E is added or removed.

interactions.mechanism: Zinc ions chelate the 4-oxo and 3-carboxyl groups of fluoroquinolones in the GI lumen, forming insoluble complexes that are not absorbed, reducing antibiotic bioavailability and risking treatment failure.
interactions.management: Take fluoroquinolone antibiotics at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after zinc supplements.
